name: Austin Safe & Sound
tagline: Safety is infrastructure. Compassion is strategy.
mission:
We advocate for evidence-based public safety in Austin — where safety means both safe public spaces for all residents and dignified, effective pathways for people in crisis. We reject the false choice between enforcement and compassion. We track outcomes, not rhetoric.

Safety is infrastructure — it's a prerequisite for everything else. You can't have thriving neighborhoods, successful transit, or family-friendly communities if people don't feel safe. The framing is dual-track and technocratic, NOT culture war:

• Some unhoused people primarily need housing and a stable environment. For them, the Housing First model works. Housing IS the intervention.

• Some unhoused people are in mental health or substance abuse crises that prevent them from maintaining housing even when offered. For them, treatment must come first or alongside housing. Offering an apartment to someone in active psychosis isn't compassionate — it's inadequate.

• Different populations need different interventions. A single ideological frame (whether "just house them" OR "just enforce the law") is guaranteed to fail because it treats a heterogeneous population as homogeneous.

• Public safety and compassion are not in tension. Residents deserve safe public spaces AND unhoused people deserve dignity and pathways to stability. Anyone who tells you these are in conflict is either selling you something or hasn't thought it through.

Austin has been through the full political cycle on this issue. In 2019, Council repealed the camping ban, allowing visible encampments. In 2021, voters passed Prop B reinstating the ban (57-43%), reflecting widespread frustration across the political spectrum. The HEAL Initiative launched — compassionately closing highest-risk encampments by offering shelter pathways, moving 635+ people from encampments to shelters since launch. By 2024, shelter capacity doubled from 708 beds to 1,657 beds, and 600+ permanent supportive housing units were added. But outcomes are mixed: HEAL data shows 48% of people who exited shelters returned to homelessness. The Marshalling Yard shelter (300 beds, 18% of total capacity) improved its successful exit rate from 15% to 30% — better, but still means 70% don't exit to permanent housing.

In February 2026, the Austin Homeless Strategy Office published its first-ever strategic plan (2025-2027) with five focus areas: system leadership, impactful investments, crisis response, health/housing exits, and community engagement. The HSO identified $101M in additional funding needs. This is the moment to establish accountability.

Austin PD is significantly understaffed relative to population — this is a well-documented, ongoing crisis. The pragmatic position is BOTH more officers (especially patrol and community policing) AND more non-police crisis response (mental health teams, HOST initiative expansion). These are complements, not substitutes. A police officer should not be the primary responder to a mental health crisis, AND there need to be enough officers to handle actual crime. The funding for both needs to come from somewhere, which connects to the fiscal accountability theme.
theory_of_change: Accountability creates pressure for improvement. When someone is watching and publicly reporting on public safety outcomes, performance improves. Austin has made significant investments in homelessness services and policing, but outcome data is fragmented, inconsistent, and often invisible to the public. We fill the accountability gap by tracking what was promised against what was delivered — across the HSO strategic plan, shelter operations, APD staffing, and HEAL encampment management. Small organized groups showing up at council with specific data and specific asks change the conversation from ideology to evidence. We make the data visible, make the tradeoffs explicit, and create political incentives for evidence-based decisions rather than performative ones.

political_positioning.description: Wide compass cloud — public safety concern is genuinely universal. This org's coalition overlaps significantly with Texas and GOP clouds (everyone wants safe neighborhoods) while also overlapping with Democratic clouds (compassionate approach, investment in social services). The overlap is the point. We reject both the abolish-the-police left and the enforcement-only right. Center-right economically on policing (fund adequate staffing), center-left on social services (invest in crisis response and shelter). Pragmatic on all of it. Broad coalition: neighborhood associations, small business owners, service providers, urbanists, parents, and anyone who wants evidence rather than ideology driving public safety policy.

key_policy_positions: Hold the HSO accountable for measurable outcomes in its 2025-2027 strategic plan across all five focus areas
Track shelter bed utilization, wait times, exit-to-housing rates, and return-to-homelessness rates by facility
Monitor APD staffing levels and 911 response times by district — make the data public and neighborhood-specific
Demand HEAL initiative transparency: track encampment outcomes, not just activity counts
Support HOST team expansion as a complement to traditional policing, not a substitute
Advocate for adequate APD patrol and community policing staffing alongside accountability reforms
Push for public dashboards on homelessness spending and outcomes — disaggregate by population and intervention type
Apply dual-track framework: Housing First for those who can maintain housing, treatment-first for those in active crisis
Bridge public safety and homelessness policy — they are the same issue viewed from different angles
Ensure crisis response includes mental health and substance abuse intervention components alongside law enforcement

active_campaigns.title: HSO Strategic Plan Accountability
active_campaigns.summary:
MAJOR UPDATE (May 17, 2026): Multiple converging crises — AISD staffing allocations completed, encampment sweeps now operational five days per week.

⚡ ENCAMPMENT SWEEPS: Operations have expanded from three to five days per week as of mid-May, using a six-step process (prioritization, outreach, 72-hour notice, closure). Only 45 shelter beds currently open despite 400+ beds added over two years. Austin's housing voucher program supporting 380+ chronically homeless residents faces cuts amid budget deficit. South Austin Housing Navigation Center (city's FIRST city-owned navigation center) operator solicitation closes soon — expected to open late summer/early fall 2026.

⚡ AISD: 215 FTE educator positions confirmed cut (85 elementary, 51 middle, 79 high school). Staffing allocations went to campuses May 8-11; leadership met with affected staff May 12-15. ⚡ Recommended budget being presented to Board TODAY (MAY 21). Final Board vote June 18 (28 DAYS). Larger class sizes and cuts to teacher planning time confirmed at all non-Band 1 campuses.

⚡ BEATF BOND PROPOSAL: Two options — $766.53M (passed unanimously, includes $131.1M for public facilities including homeless services) and $436M alternative. ⚡ NEW: Council members floated a $353M parks-focused counter-proposal at May 20 work session, EXCLUDING housing. Staff recommends delay to 2028. BEATF presents to Council May 28 (7 DAYS). For homelessness services, the $131.1M public facilities allocation in the full package would fund shelter infrastructure — the parks-only version loses this.

⚡ OC PARALLEL: Orange County's 2026 PIT count results (published May 19) show a 13.7% decrease in homelessness (6,321 total), with more people in shelters than on streets for the first time. But less than 1 in 5 shelter residents exit to permanent housing — the same fundamental challenge Austin faces. OC's data provides a useful benchmark for Austin's accountability framework.

⚡ BUDGET CRISIS: $26.4M projected deficit. $16.8M in social services cuts — targeting permanent supportive housing, workforce development, adult education. THIS DIRECTLY THREATENS HSO STRATEGIC PLAN FUNDING.

Strategic plan commitments (adopted March 26):
• 650 new shelter beds + 2 navigation centers (first in procurement)
• $101M additional funding ask — NOW THREATENED by $16.8M cuts
• ~6,500 people on streets
• Endeavors operates ALL major shelters under emergency contract through September 2026
• Competitive solicitation for shelter operations starts October 1, 2026

**May 23 update:** Council approved the Density Bonus Program on May 22 — expanding housing production tools that feed the HSO's permanent housing exit strategy. The 2,600-acre Dog's Head annexation was also approved — both increase the long-term housing pipeline. BEATF bond presentation ($766.5M or $353M parks-only alternative) goes to council May 28 (5 DAYS). For homelessness services, the full package includes $131.1M in public facilities that could fund shelter infrastructure — the parks-only version loses this. ⚡ AISD recommended budget being presented to Board — 215 FTE educator cuts confirmed. Final Board vote June 18 (26 DAYS). The budget crisis deepens: $26.4M projected deficit, $16.8M in social services cuts directly threatening HSO strategic plan funding.

**May 24 update:** BEATF bond presentation May 28 (4 DAYS) — the full $766.5M package includes $131.1M for public facilities including homeless services infrastructure. The $353M parks-only counter-proposal excludes this. Pct 4 runoff Election Day May 26 (2 DAYS). AISD board final vote on budget June 18 (25 DAYS) — 215 FTE educator cuts and larger class sizes now the formal proposal.

**May 25 update:** BEATF bond presentation May 28 (3 DAYS) — the full $766.5M package includes $131.1M for public facilities (shelter infrastructure, community centers). The $353M parks-only counter-proposal EXCLUDES this. ⚡ Pct 4 runoff TOMORROW (May 26). ⚡ AISD budget draft DELAYED to June 4 — Superintendent Segura identified $130M in cuts but needs ~$50M more. The $181M deficit creates cascading effects: 215 FTE educator cuts reduce school quality, which drives family flight to suburbs, which reduces enrollment, which worsens the deficit. Board must pass budget by end of June. Encampment management teams continue operating five days/week citywide — only 45 shelter beds currently available despite 400+ added over two years. South Austin Housing Navigation Center operator solicitation pending.

**May 26 update:** ⚡ Pct 4 runoff is TODAY. BEATF bond presentation May 28 (2 DAYS) — the full $766.5M package includes $131.1M for public facilities (shelter infrastructure). AISD budget draft now expected June 4 (9 DAYS). Encampment management teams continue operating five days/week citywide. Only 45 shelter beds currently available.

**May 27 update:** ⚡ Pct 4 runoff results: George Morales won ~55% — notably, Morales is a former constable with law enforcement background, directly relevant to public safety coordination at the county level. TOMORROW (May 28) council agenda includes $156.3M Certificates of Obligation for fire stations and public safety facilities, plus the BEATF bond presentation (the full $766.5M package includes $131.1M for public facilities including shelter infrastructure; staff recommend delay to 2028; CMs Ellis & Laine favor ~$350M smaller bond). Also on the May 28 agenda: e-moto regulation resolution addressing sidewalk and street safety. ⚡ Severe thunderstorms expected TONIGHT — monitor impact on encampment populations and emergency response capacity with only 45 shelter beds available.

⚡ MAY 28 UPDATE: Council agenda includes $156.3M Certificates of Obligation for fire stations, EMS facilities, and a family violence project. BEATF presented $766.5M bond (includes $131.1M for public facilities — community centers, animal shelter, homeless services infrastructure). I-35 Cap & Stitch $104M advance funding approved. Severe thunderstorm aftermath — monitor impact on encampment populations with only 45 shelter beds available. HSO encampment management teams continuing five days/week citywide with 42-member staff across six teams.

**May 29 update:** ⚡ BOND DECISION DELAYED: Council pushed the bond vote to July. Mayor Watson opposes a 2026 bond ('not yet'), citing financial policies and failed November tax rate election. The full $766.5M BEATF package includes $131.1M for public facilities (shelter infrastructure, community centers, animal shelter) — but the ~$350M parks-focused alternative EXCLUDES this. If the smaller bond wins or the bond is delayed to 2028, HSO loses a critical shelter infrastructure funding source. ⚡ $156.3M Certificates of Obligation approved for fire/EMS stations, family violence shelter, and public safety facilities — this is a separate funding mechanism from the bond. ⚡ Encampment management teams continue five days/week citywide — only 45 shelter beds available. The housing voucher program supporting 380+ chronically homeless residents faces cuts amid the $26.4M general fund deficit. ⚡ Austin Energy peaker unit plan ($1B, 400MW gas + 100MW battery + 299MW wind) approved May 21 — controversial executive session process sparked transparency backlash. ⚡ AISD budget draft delayed to June 4 (6 DAYS). The $181M deficit creates cascading effects on family stability and neighborhood quality.

**May 30 update:** ⚡ BOND PROCEEDS BUT SHELTER FUNDING AT RISK: Council voted 6-2-1 on May 29 to proceed with ~$390M bond development (parks, transportation, community facilities) — overruling Watson's opposition. BUT the $131.1M public facilities allocation that could fund shelter infrastructure is NOT in the current $390M direction. The July 23 final vote is the last chance to advocate for adding public facilities funding. ⚡ Council also approved oversight guidelines for the ~$1B gas peaker plant, including emissions caps and restricted use of older units at Decker Creek — relevant to environmental health in encampment-adjacent areas. ⚡ AISD budget draft expected June 4 (5 DAYS). Board final vote June 18 (19 DAYS). The ~$50M gap between identified cuts ($152.7M) and the full $181M deficit remains unresolved. Encampment management teams continue five days/week — only 45 shelter beds available.

**May 31 update:** AISD budget proposal expected June 4 (4 DAYS) — the $181M deficit and librarian-splitting across campuses compound social services strain. Board final vote June 18 (18 DAYS). The ~$390M bond direction (parks, transportation, community facilities) does NOT include shelter infrastructure. The $101M HSO funding ask and shelter investments depend entirely on FY2027 operating budget — which faces $26.4M deficit and $16.8M social services cuts. South Austin Housing Navigation Center solicitation still pending. Encampment management teams continue five days/week. Statewide: Vikki Goodwin won the Democratic LG runoff (68%). Bond final vote July 23 (53 DAYS) — the advocacy window for adding public facilities funding back is NOW.

**June 1 update:** AT-Home Initiative proposals due TOMORROW (June 2) — $6.7M, 5-year effort for permanent supportive housing case management. City and Travis County to award up to 3 provider contracts starting September 2026. 98% of people in permanent supportive housing remain housed. City also partnered with Housing Connector and Zillow to expand housing access. Key accountability metric: does AT-Home contract selection prioritize evidence-based providers?

**June 3 update:** George Morales won Pct 4 commissioner runoff ~55% (May 26) — first new commissioner in 30+ years, brings law enforcement background (former constable) and infrastructure focus. Council approved $156.3M Certificates of Obligation for fire/EMS stations, family violence shelter, and public safety facilities (May 28). Gas peaker plant oversight guidelines approved (May 29). AISD budget crisis ($181M deficit, 215 educator cuts) intersects with public safety through school resource officers and community investment. Council in summer recess until July 23.

**June 4 update:** AISD budget proposal presented today — 558 positions, 11 school closures, school police reductions. Morales appointment June 11 adds law enforcement perspective to Commissioners Court. AT-Home Initiative proposals due June 2 ($6.7M).

**June 5 update:** AISD budget vote June 18 (13 DAYS) — 558 positions affected, 11 school closures, school police reductions. School closures concentrate neighborhood instability. AT-Home Initiative proposals ($6.7M, 5-year) submitted June 2 — contract awards expected September 2026. George Morales formally appointed Pct 4 commissioner June 11 (6 DAYS) — brings law enforcement background. Bond final vote July 23 — shelter infrastructure ($131.1M public facilities) NOT in current ~$390M direction. Council on summer recess.

**June 7 update:** AISD parents/teachers protested budget cuts June 5. The $181M deficit plan (558 positions, 11 school closures) is heading for board vote June 18. School closures and larger class sizes compound public safety dynamics — reduced school investment can increase at-risk youth population. The HSO strategic plan's 5 focus areas remain the framework for accountability. Council returns from recess July 23.

**June 8 update:** South Austin Housing Navigation Center RFP and AT-Home Initiative ($6.7M, 5-year) proposals both closed June 2 — evaluation underway. Up to three providers will be selected; contracts start September 2026. Navigation Center — the city's FIRST city-owned facility — expected to open late summer/early fall 2026. AISD board vote June 18 (10 DAYS) — 558 positions affected, 11 schools closing. New transportation hub model proposed. School closures compound homelessness risk. Bond final vote July 23 — shelter infrastructure NOT in ~$390M direction.

**June 9 update:** AT-Home Initiative ($6.7M, 5-year) and Navigation Center RFP evaluations continue. A 13-member Center Advisory Board is being formed (5 local reps, 3 people with lived experience, 4 housing/service partners, 1 civic rep). Navigation Center on track for late summer/early fall opening — the city's FIRST city-owned facility. AISD board vote June 18 (9 DAYS) — 558 positions, 11 school closures compound homelessness risk. Bond final vote July 23 (44 DAYS) — shelter infrastructure NOT in ~$390M direction.

**June 10 update:** AT-Home Initiative ($6.7M, 5-year) proposals closed June 2 — city in review/selection phase. Up to 3 providers selected; contracts start September 2026. Navigation Center on track for late summer/early fall opening. 13-member Advisory Board formed. AISD full cut plan released June 3: 215 educator salaries cut, 3,000 students lost, protest rally June 5. Board vote June 18 (8 DAYS) — school closures compound homelessness risk. Bond final vote July 23 (43 DAYS) — shelter infrastructure NOT in ~$390M direction. Morales sworn in TOMORROW (June 11).

**June 11 update:** AT-Home Initiative — city in review/selection phase. Permanent supportive housing has 98% retention rate. Navigation Center on track for late summer/early fall opening; Advisory Board formed. Morales sworn in as Pct 4 commissioner TODAY — county budget votes influence homelessness infrastructure. AISD board vote June 18 (7 DAYS) — 558 positions, 11 school closures compound homelessness risk. Bond final vote July 23 (42 DAYS) — shelter infrastructure NOT in ~$390M direction.

**June 12 update:** AT-Home Initiative — city continues review/selection phase. Navigation Center on track for late summer/early fall opening. Morales began first full day as Pct 4 commissioner. ⚡ AISD deficit ballooned to $95M — board may delay June 18 vote to June 25; district needs September loan for payroll. School closures compound homelessness risk. Bond final vote July 23 (41 DAYS) — shelter infrastructure NOT in ~$390M direction.

I-35 on track for late summer/early fall; operator proposals solicited (up to $250K). 13-member Advisory Board formed from 69 applications. AISD board vote on $181M shortfall June 18 (5 DAYS) — school closures compound homelessness risk. Bond final vote July 23 (40 DAYS) — shelter infrastructure NOT in $390M direction.

**June 14 update:** AT-Home Initiative — city in review/selection phase. Navigation Center at 2401 S. I-35 on track for late summer/early fall; operator proposals solicited (up to $250K). **June 15 update:** AT-Home Initiative — city in review/selection phase. Navigation Center at 2401 S. I-35 on track for late summer/early fall. AISD board vote on $181M shortfall June 18 (3 DAYS) — school closures compound homelessness risk. Bond final vote July 23 — shelter infrastructure NOT in $390M direction.

**June 16 update:** AT-Home Initiative — city in review/selection phase. Navigation Center at 2401 S. I-35 on track for late summer/early fall. AISD board vote on $181M shortfall June 18 (2 DAYS) — school closures compound homelessness risk. Bond final vote July 23 — shelter infrastructure NOT in $390M direction.

**June 17 update:** South Austin Navigation Center at 2401 S. I-35 — RFP open for operator, up to $250K; contracts for up to 3 providers, 12-month term beginning September 2026. AT-Home Initiative ($6.7M, 5-year) — proposals due June 2, city in review. AISD board vote TOMORROW (June 18) — school closures compound homelessness risk. Bond final vote July 23 — shelter infrastructure NOT in $390M direction. Travis County offices CLOSED for Juneteenth.

**June 19 update:** Juneteenth — offices CLOSED. ⚡ AISD budget approved 7-1 at $205M — 10 school closures and 558 position cuts compound homelessness risk for families. South Austin Navigation Center on track for late summer/early fall opening. AT-Home Initiative proposals under review — contracts for up to 3 providers, September 2026 start.

**June 20 update:** ⚡ Sunrise Homeless Navigation Center selected as tentative operator for the South Austin Housing Navigation Center at 2401 S. I-35 — the city's FIRST city-owned navigation center. Sunrise has rehoused 800+ people in 2026. Council must approve the recommendation on July 23. 13-member Advisory Board formed from 69 applicants. Bond final vote July 23 — shelter infrastructure NOT in ~$390M direction.

Sunrise stats: 800+ rehoused in 2026, 1,880 connected to housing in 2025, 28,801 served total. AT-Home Initiative ($6.7M, 5-year) proposals under review. Raising Travis County $17.65M expansion continues serving 5,200+ children through after-school programs. Council on recess until July 23 (30 DAYS).

CDBG comment through July 20; hearing July 14 (15 DAYS). City Manager budget July 16 (17 DAYS).

**June 30 update:** SAHNC opening June 2027. Construction at 2401 S. I-35 through spring 2027. Sunrise tentative operator — council vote July 23 (23 DAYS). Sunrise rehoused 800 in 2026. AT-Home Initiative awards September. Raising Travis County: $17.34M for 1,000 childcare scholarships + $4.16M provider quality improvements. Bond: shelter NOT in ~$390M direction. CDBG comment through July 20; hearing July 14 (14 DAYS). City Manager budget July 16 (16 DAYS).
active_campaigns.theory_of_change: Strategic plans without accountability mechanisms become performative documents. The HSO plan is the city's first-ever comprehensive homelessness strategy — if it succeeds, it's a model. If it fails quietly, Austin loses years. Public tracking with quarterly reports creates three pressure points: (1) city staff know someone is watching, (2) council members have data for budget decisions, (3) residents can see whether their tax dollars are producing results. This is the most leveraged campaign because it covers all homelessness spending and strategy, not just individual programs.
next_actions: Obtain and analyze the complete HSO 2025-2027 strategic plan — identify every measurable commitment and timeline
Build a public tracking dashboard mapping each of the five focus areas to specific metrics and deadlines
Attend HSO community engagement sessions and council briefings — establish presence as consistent accountability voice
Submit public records requests for baseline data on each strategic plan metric
Publish first quarterly progress report by Q2 2026
Identify which of the $101M funding gaps have been addressed and which remain
Present accountability findings at council budget hearings to inform FY2027 appropriations

bridge_building.opposition_steelman: Reasonable skeptics of HSO Strategic Plan Accountability often worry about unintended costs, fairness across neighborhoods, and whether promised benefits will be measured transparently rather than assumed.

cross_partisan_appeals.framing: Define clear outcomes, timelines, and guardrails before scaling spending or mandates.
cross_partisan_appeals.evidence: Campaigns with published milestones and public reporting earn broader support and allow mid-course corrections.
cross_partisan_appeals.example_dialogue: "If HSO Strategic Plan Accountability is worth doing, it is worth measuring with transparent benchmarks so everyone can see what is working."

cross_partisan_appeals.framing: Implement change in ways that reduce disruption and share benefits across existing residents and newcomers.
cross_partisan_appeals.evidence: Phased implementation and regular public feedback often lower backlash while improving policy durability.
cross_partisan_appeals.example_dialogue: "We can move HSO Strategic Plan Accountability forward while still listening to neighborhood-level concerns and adjusting implementation details."

common_ground: Public policy should solve real problems, not just signal values.
Residents deserve clear communication about tradeoffs.
Programs should be adjusted when evidence shows gaps.
conversation_traps_to_avoid: Assuming all critics are acting in bad faith.
Overpromising speed or certainty on complex implementation.
Using national partisan frames for local decisions.
when_to_acknowledge_uncertainty: When baseline data is incomplete or lagged.
When legal or budget decisions are still pending.
When implementation depends on partner agencies outside our control.
honest_limits: This campaign cannot alone fix every upstream structural problem.
We cannot guarantee identical outcomes in every neighborhood.
We rely on sustained public participation and institutional follow-through.

active_campaigns.title: Shelter Capacity & Outcomes Tracking
active_campaigns.summary:
UPDATE (March 2026): Major shelter operator consolidation — Endeavors now runs ALL major shelters: Marshalling Yard, Northbridge, ARCH, and Eighth Street downtown shelters under emergency contract through September 2026. Competitive solicitation for operations planned October 1, 2026. Director Gray has proposed a bond project to replace the Marshalling Yard with a new purpose-built shelter, potentially consolidating ARCH and Eighth Street shelters as well.

Austin doubled shelter beds to 1,657 but the Marshalling Yard exit rate is still only 30% — meaning 70% of residents don't exit to permanent housing. With the new strategic plan calling for 650 additional beds, the question shifts: will more beds with the same programming produce better outcomes, or does the model need to change? HEAL data shows 48% of people who exited shelters returned to homelessness — that's a revolving door.

Track bed utilization, wait times, exit rates, and return-to-homelessness rates by facility — now all under one operator (Endeavors), which should make data comparison easier. Disaggregate by population type. Identify which programs produce the best outcomes and advocate for scaling those models.

**June 1 update:** HEAL expanding to 42 staff across 6 full-time encampment teams. 835+ individuals moved from high-risk encampments to shelters since HEAL began in 2021. Advocacy groups criticize that shelters are full and HSO is prioritizing sweeps over housing. AT-Home Initiative ($6.7M, proposals due June 2) aims to address the shelter-to-permanent-housing pipeline.

**June 4 update:** AT-Home Initiative proposals submitted June 2. $6.7M, 5-year program for permanent supportive housing services.

**June 5 update:** AT-Home Initiative proposals ($6.7M, 5-year) under review — contract awards expected September 2026. Endeavors emergency shelter contract runs through September 2026; competitive solicitation for operations starts October 1. South Austin Housing Navigation Center operator solicitation pending. Council on summer recess until July 23.

**June 22 update:** Sunrise Homeless Navigation Center selected as tentative operator for South Austin Navigation Center — council vote July 23 (31 DAYS). AT-Home Initiative ($6.7M, 5-year) proposals under review; contracts for up to 3 providers start September 2026. Endeavors emergency shelter contract runs through September 2026; competitive solicitation October 1. Marshalling Yard exit rate still 30% — the fundamental outcomes question persists. Bond survey closes TOMORROW — shelter infrastructure NOT in ~$390M direction. Council on recess until July 23.

June 23 update: Shelter beds at 1,657 (doubled since 2024). Endeavors emergency contract continues through September 2026; competitive solicitation starts October 1. AT-Home Initiative ($6.7M, 5-year) proposals under review; contract awards expected September 2026. Sunrise Navigation Center (2401 S. I-35) on track for late summer/early fall 2026. Council on recess until July 23 (30 DAYS).

**June 27 update:** SAHNC opening delayed to June 2027. Marshalling Yard: up to 300 clients (Family Endeavors). Southbridge: new contract active since Feb 27 ($4M). HSO Director Gray acknowledged 'not enough shelter beds for everyone living unhoused in Austin.' 6 HEM teams (42 staff) being stood up for encampment management. Council on recess until July 23 (26 DAYS).

**June 28 update:** SAHNC (2401 S. I-35) facility expected to open June 2027 (slipped from late summer 2026). Sunrise named tentative operator; city offering $250K/year. AT-Home Initiative ($6.7M, 5-year): proposals submitted June 2, under review — up to 3 providers selected. ⚡ VOCAL-TX vigil TOMORROW (June 29). Council on recess until July 23 (25 DAYS).
active_campaigns.theory_of_change: Shelter is the most visible and politically charged piece of homelessness infrastructure. Currently, the public debate is about bed counts — how many beds exist. But bed counts without outcome data are meaningless if 70% of residents cycle back to homelessness. Shifting the conversation from capacity to outcomes changes what success looks like and creates pressure to fund evidence-based programs. Facility-level data identifies what's working (scale it) and what's not (fix it or reallocate). Disaggregating by population type (economic crisis vs. chronic illness vs. substance abuse) enables the dual-track approach in practice.
next_actions: Submit public records requests for bed utilization rates, length of stay, and exit outcomes at each major shelter facility
Partner with ECHO for HMIS data on return-to-homelessness rates by shelter and program type
Research Marshalling Yard outcomes in detail — what changed to improve exit rates from 15% to 30%, and what would get to 50%
Build facility-level outcomes dashboard: utilization, exit-to-housing rate, return rate, average length of stay
Identify which shelter programs serve which populations — disaggregate outcomes by population type
Publish first shelter outcomes report comparing facility performance
Present findings to council with specific recommendations for scaling high-performing programs

bridge_building.opposition_steelman: Reasonable skeptics of Shelter Capacity & Outcomes Tracking often worry about unintended costs, fairness across neighborhoods, and whether promised benefits will be measured transparently rather than assumed.

cross_partisan_appeals.framing: Define clear outcomes, timelines, and guardrails before scaling spending or mandates.
cross_partisan_appeals.evidence: Campaigns with published milestones and public reporting earn broader support and allow mid-course corrections.
cross_partisan_appeals.example_dialogue: "If Shelter Capacity & Outcomes Tracking is worth doing, it is worth measuring with transparent benchmarks so everyone can see what is working."

cross_partisan_appeals.framing: Implement change in ways that reduce disruption and share benefits across existing residents and newcomers.
cross_partisan_appeals.evidence: Phased implementation and regular public feedback often lower backlash while improving policy durability.
cross_partisan_appeals.example_dialogue: "We can move Shelter Capacity & Outcomes Tracking forward while still listening to neighborhood-level concerns and adjusting implementation details."

common_ground: Public policy should solve real problems, not just signal values.
Residents deserve clear communication about tradeoffs.
Programs should be adjusted when evidence shows gaps.
conversation_traps_to_avoid: Assuming all critics are acting in bad faith.
Overpromising speed or certainty on complex implementation.
Using national partisan frames for local decisions.
when_to_acknowledge_uncertainty: When baseline data is incomplete or lagged.
When legal or budget decisions are still pending.
When implementation depends on partner agencies outside our control.
honest_limits: This campaign cannot alone fix every upstream structural problem.
We cannot guarantee identical outcomes in every neighborhood.
We rely on sustained public participation and institutional follow-through.

active_campaigns.title: APD Staffing & Response Time Monitoring
active_campaigns.summary:
UPDATE (May 15, 2026): APD staffing shows incremental progress but the 19% vacancy rate persists. Key numbers:

• 340 vacancies out of 1,816 authorized positions (19% vacancy rate)
• FY2026 budget: $540M (36.2% of city's $1.5B general fund)
• New $218M APA contract provides 28% pay raise over 5 years
• Three cadet classes graduated since last spring — 122 total new officers
• 156th cadet class: 51 cadets, graduating August 2026
• 157th class began April 21 with 65 cadets — scaling toward 100-person classes
• Attrition slowing: net gain of 3 officers in 2025 (first positive year in 5-6 years)
• Recruitment interest up 166% per Chief Davis; Chief says fully staffed by end of 2027
• APA President Bullock: 'still about 700 officers short' when accounting for population growth

POSITIVE SIGNALS:
• For the first time since August 2023, APD met its target response time of ≤10:44 for Priority 0 and Priority 1 calls.
• Integral Care is hiring 23 more mental health specialists for 24/7 crisis response.
• May 7: Council approved $497K for Integral Care's Homeless Health and Wellness Center — expanding crisis health services and non-police response capacity.

⚡ MAY 5 WORK SESSION: HSO Director Gray presented the sweeps expansion plan to Council. The HEM team expansion means two APD officers per encampment team — 12 officers total diverted to sweep operations. With 340 vacancies and 700+ monthly 311 calls, monitor whether this pulls patrol resources and affects response times.

BUDGET RISK: FY2027 budget talks ongoing. $26.4M deficit. General fund growing only ~3%. No civilian pay raises. While APD's $540M budget is the largest single allocation, fiscal pressure could affect staffing pipeline. AISD budget update (May 5): $73.8M in central cuts, $33.9M campus cuts identified. Staffing allocations to campuses May 8-11. Next: May 9 virtual budget meeting, May 21 recommended budget.

⚡ BEATF BOND PROPOSAL: The $766.53M bond package does not directly fund police staffing but the $131.1M public facilities allocation and general fiscal relief could reduce budget pressure on the general fund. BEATF presents to Council May 28 (8 DAYS). Late BEATF discussions prioritized East Austin priorities (Carver Museum, Colony Park). Task force recommended 'contract with the voters' for accountability.

⚡ MAY 28 CONVERGENCE: Three major items land on the same day — BEATF bond presentation to Council, Project Connect funding trial in Travis County District Court (May 28-30), and the new Texas AV regulation law takes effect. Watch for budget and staffing implications across all three.

**May 18 update:** ⚡ TWELVE SHOOTINGS across Austin on May 17 — four people wounded, two teen suspects and a third arrested. The incidents spanned multiple neighborhoods and strained APD response capacity. This follows the March 1 Sixth Street mass shooting (3 dead, 13+ injured). Despite APD meeting Priority 0/1 response time targets for the first time since August 2023, the May 17 spree demonstrates that recovery is fragile with 340 vacancies. The 157th cadet class (65 cadets) began April 21 but full staffing isn't expected until end of 2027. Waymo also recalled 3,791 robotaxis nationally after a San Antonio flooding incident — the new TX DMV enforcement authority takes effect May 28.

**May 27 update:** ⚡ Pct 4 runoff results: George Morales (former constable, ~55%) won over Ledesma-Woody (~45%) — his law enforcement background is significant for public safety coordination at the county level. TOMORROW's council agenda (May 28) includes $156.3M Certificates of Obligation funding fire stations and public safety facilities, plus the e-moto regulation resolution addressing sidewalk/street safety. Severe thunderstorms expected TONIGHT — potential strain on emergency response with 340 APD vacancies.

⚡ MAY 28 UPDATE: $156.3M Certificates of Obligation for fire stations and public safety facilities on today's council agenda. 156th cadet class (49 cadets) graduated May 1. 157th class (65 cadets) began January 26, graduation expected September 18. 340 vacancies remain (19% vacancy rate) — Chief Davis still targeting fully staffed by end of 2027.

**May 29 update:** ⚡ $156.3M Certificates of Obligation approved for fire/EMS stations, administrative office buildings, and public safety facilities — separate from the bond debate. Bond decision itself delayed to July. The 157th cadet class (65 cadets, graduation expected September 18) continues. 340 vacancies remain (19% vacancy rate).

**June 1 update:** APD still ~700 officers short but recruitment applications up 166% following $218M police union contract. Department gained 3 net officers last year — first positive year in recent history. Chief Davis projects full staffing by end of 2027. City audit (April 2025) found APD lacks a long-term recruitment plan. AISD budget proposal (June 4) includes reducing police officers at schools.

**June 4 update:** APD 'Coffee with a Cop' event downtown today. ~330 sworn officer vacancies (KXAN). Chief Davis projects full staffing by end of 2027. AISD budget proposal would reduce school police officers.

**June 5 update:** ~330 sworn officer vacancies (slightly improved from 340). 157th cadet class (65 cadets, started January 26) on track for September 18 graduation. 156th class graduated May 1 (49 cadets). Net staffing trajectory positive — first net gain in 5-6 years in 2025. $218M APA contract driving 166% increase in recruitment interest. AISD budget vote June 18 (13 DAYS) — school police reductions may create spillover demand for APD. Council on summer recess.

**June 17 update:** 157th cadet class mid-training (started Jan 26) — graduation September 18. APD remains 300+ officers short. AISD $181M shortfall includes campus police cuts — board vote TOMORROW (June 18). Council on recess until July 23.

**June 18 update:** 157th cadet class mid-training (started Jan 26) — graduation September 18. APD remains 300+ officers short (~1,819 of ~2,120 authorized); annual budget $527.9M. AISD board voting TODAY on $181M shortfall — includes campus police cuts. Council on recess until July 23. Travis County offices CLOSED for Juneteenth.

**June 19 update:** Juneteenth — offices CLOSED. ⚡ AISD budget approved 7-1 at $205M — includes campus police cuts. APD remains 300+ officers short (~1,819 of ~2,120 authorized). 157th cadet class graduation September 18. Council on recess until July 23.

**June 20 update:** 157th cadet class mid-training — graduation September 18. APD remains 300+ officers short (~1,819 of ~2,120 authorized). AISD campus police cuts could increase demand on APD patrol resources. Council on recess until July 23.

**June 21 update:** 157th cadet class mid-training — graduation September 18. 156th class graduated May 1. APD remains 300+ officers short (~1,819 of ~2,120 authorized). AISD campus police cuts from $205M budget increase demand on APD. Council on recess until July 23 (32 DAYS).

**June 22 update:** 157th cadet class mid-training — graduation September 18. APD remains 300+ officers short. AISD boundary workshops TODAY — 10 school closures and campus police cuts from $205M budget increase demand on APD patrol resources as school zones are redrawn. Bond survey closes TOMORROW — bond final vote July 23 (31 DAYS). Council on recess until July 23 (31 DAYS). Filing period for council races opens July 19 (27 DAYS) — public safety staffing will be a defining campaign issue.

June 23 update: APD vacancy rate ~19% (330-340 of ~1,816 authorized). 157th cadet class (65 cadets) on track for September 18 graduation. APD met Priority 0/1 response time target for first time since August 2023. Recruitment applications up 166% following $218M police union contract. AISD campus police cuts from $205M budget may increase APD patrol demand. Council on recess until July 23 (30 DAYS).

**June 24 update:** 157th cadet class mid-training — graduation September 18. APD remains 300+ officers short (~1,819 of ~2,120 authorized). Council on recess until July 23 (29 DAYS).

**June 25 update:** 157th cadet class mid-training — graduation September 18. APD remains 300+ officers short (~1,819 of ~2,120 authorized). Austin's 30th homicide of 2026 recorded June 24 on South Lamar. Council on recess until July 23 (28 DAYS).

**June 26 update:** 30+ homicides in 2026 — tracking slightly below 2025 pace. APD has 330+ sworn officer vacancies (~1,819 of ~2,120 authorized). Chief Davis says staffing will stabilize in 2-3 years. 157th cadet class mid-training. Council on recess until July 23 (27 DAYS).

**June 27 update:** ⚡ 31+ homicides — 5 in 2 weeks in mid-to-late June (APD says unconnected). 330+ sworn officer vacancies. POSITIVE: net 3 officers gained this year — first positive gain in years. Applications up 166%. Chief Davis projects full staffing by end of 2027. Next academy applications due September 16. ⚡ KUT investigation: council voted on ~$1B peaker plant in secret. Council on recess until July 23 (26 DAYS).

**June 28 update:** 31+ homicides in 2026 as of June 24. APD has 330+ sworn officer vacancies. ⚡ Positive: net gain of 3 officers this year — first positive gain in years. Applications up 166%. Chief Davis projects full staffing by end of 2027. Next academy deadline September 16. Council on recess until July 23 (25 DAYS).

**June 29 update:** 30+ homicides in 2026 — 5 in 2 weeks in late June; tracking slightly below 2025 pace. 300+ sworn vacancies. First net positive officer gain in years. Chief Davis: staffing stabilizes in 2-3 years. City Manager budget presentation July 16 (17 DAYS). Council on recess until July 23 (24 DAYS).

**June 30 update:** 30+ homicides in 2026 — tracking below 2025 pace (55 total, vs 72 in 2024). 300+ sworn vacancies. First net positive officer gain in years. Chief Davis: staffing stabilizes in 2-3 years. City Manager budget presentation July 16 (16 DAYS). Council on recess until July 23 (23 DAYS).
active_campaigns.theory_of_change: Response time data by district makes the staffing crisis concrete and neighborhood-specific rather than abstract and citywide. Council members cannot ignore data showing their district has deteriorating response times — it's the kind of constituent concern that drives budget votes. Simultaneously, tracking HOST and mental health team diversion rates demonstrates the value of non-police response: every mental health call handled by HOST is a call that doesn't require a patrol officer. This creates evidence for funding BOTH more officers AND more crisis teams, breaking the false binary. District-level data also reveals equity issues — which neighborhoods bear the worst of the staffing crisis.
next_actions: Submit public records requests for APD sworn officer counts, vacancy rates, and cadet class sizes over the past 3 years
Obtain 911 response time data by district and priority level — identify neighborhoods with worst response times
Research HOST team capacity: how many calls diverted from patrol, what outcomes, what expansion plans
Track mental health crisis response: how many calls, who responds, what outcomes compared to patrol response
Build district-level APD staffing and response time dashboard
Publish first APD staffing report with district-level response time analysis
Present response time data at council budget hearings — connect staffing levels to constituent safety outcomes

bridge_building.opposition_steelman: Reasonable skeptics of APD Staffing & Response Time Monitoring often worry about unintended costs, fairness across neighborhoods, and whether promised benefits will be measured transparently rather than assumed.

active_campaigns.title: HEAL Initiative Transparency
active_campaigns.summary:
HEAL (Homeless Encampment Assistance Link) has been the primary mechanism for encampment management in Austin since 2021, moving 635+ people from encampments to shelters. But transparency on outcomes is limited. Track which encampments are targeted, what services are offered, how many people accept shelter, how many achieve permanent housing, and how many return to homelessness.

This is accountability work on an initiative that both sides of the debate should want to be transparent. Advocates for unhoused people want to ensure HEAL is actually connecting people to services, not just displacing them. Residents who want encampments addressed want to know that HEAL operations produce lasting results, not just temporary relocations. Whether the city is meeting its own stated criteria for 'compassionate' closure should be publicly verifiable.

**June 1 update:** HEAL expanding to 42 staff across 6 full-time teams (North/South/Central Austin + corridors/waterways/litter). 835+ individuals moved from encampments to shelters since 2021 launch. Advocacy groups raising concerns about sweeps over housing. Key transparency questions: housing outcomes for HEAL-moved individuals? AT-Home Initiative ($6.7M, proposals due June 2) designed to address this gap.

**June 4 update:** AT-Home Initiative proposals submitted June 2. $6.7M, 5-year program for permanent supportive housing services.

**June 5 update:** HEAL teams continue operating five days/week citywide with 42 staff across 6 teams. AT-Home Initiative ($6.7M) proposals submitted June 2 — designed to address the shelter-to-permanent-housing pipeline gap that HEAL critics highlight. Only ~45 shelter beds currently available. Council on summer recess. Monitor for AT-Home contract awards (expected September 2026).

**June 22 update:** HEAL teams continue five days/week operations with 42 staff across 6 teams. AT-Home Initiative ($6.7M, 5-year) proposals under review — contract awards for up to 3 providers expected September 2026, designed to address the shelter-to-permanent-housing pipeline. Sunrise selected as tentative Navigation Center operator — council vote July 23 (31 DAYS). AISD boundary workshops TODAY — school closures compound neighborhood instability. CDBG public comment through July 20; hearing July 14 at 9 AM (22 DAYS). Council on recess until July 23.

June 23 update: HEAL teams operating 5 days/week with 42 staff across 6 teams. AT-Home Initiative ($6.7M) designed to address shelter-to-housing pipeline; proposals under review, contract awards expected September 2026. Only ~45 shelter beds currently available despite 400+ added. Bond survey closed today — shelter infrastructure not in bond direction. Council on recess until July 23 (30 DAYS).

**June 28 update:** HEAL Initiative monitoring continues. ⚡ KUT investigation (June 26) revealed council has voted secretly on city matters since 1999 — transparency advocacy extends beyond HEAL to Austin Energy oversight. Council on recess until July 23 (25 DAYS).
active_campaigns.theory_of_change: HEAL operates as the city's primary encampment intervention but with limited public outcome reporting. The initiative reports activity counts (encampments closed, people contacted) but not long-term outcomes (permanent housing placement, return to homelessness). Sunshine is the best disinfectant — publishing what actually happens during and after HEAL operations forces the program to demonstrate lasting results. This accountability serves both political flanks: those who support encampment management get evidence it works, those who oppose displacement get evidence of whether alternatives are offered. Transparent data de-polarizes the debate by replacing ideology with outcomes.
next_actions: Submit public records requests for HEAL operation data: encampments targeted, people contacted, services offered, shelter placements
Track post-HEAL outcomes: of people who accepted shelter, how many achieved permanent housing within 6 and 12 months
Attend HEAL community notification meetings to understand the process and document offers made
Research whether HEAL operations meet the city's own stated criteria for compassionate encampment closure
Compare HEAL outcomes across different encampment types and locations
Publish HEAL transparency report with operation-level outcome data

bridge_building.opposition_steelman: Reasonable skeptics of HEAL Initiative Transparency often worry about unintended costs, fairness across neighborhoods, and whether promised benefits will be measured transparently rather than assumed.

aligned_organizations.name: Integral Care
aligned_organizations.relationship: Travis County's mental health authority and key partner on crisis response. Essential data source for understanding non-police intervention outcomes and mental health crisis response capacity. Their expansion directly reduces demand on APD for mental health calls.

aligned_organizations.name: Austin Area Urban League
aligned_organizations.relationship: Operates Southbridge shelter. Aligned on equity dimensions of public safety and homelessness policy. Community credibility in East Austin communities disproportionately affected by both crime and homelessness.

aligned_organizations.name: Family Endeavors
aligned_organizations.relationship: Major homelessness service provider operating the Marshalling Yard and Northbridge shelters. Essential data partner for shelter outcomes tracking. Their program outcomes are central to our shelter accountability campaign.

aligned_organizations.name: ECHO
aligned_organizations.relationship: Ending Community Homelessness Coalition — administers the Homeless Management Information System (HMIS) and Point-in-Time count. Essential data and coordination partner for all homelessness outcome tracking.

aligned_organizations.name: Austin Monitor
aligned_organizations.relationship: Independent local news source covering city government. Key information source for tracking council actions on public safety budgets, HSO operations, and APD staffing decisions.

aligned_organizations.name: Austin Bulldog
aligned_organizations.relationship: Investigative local journalism. Information source for government accountability on public safety spending and homelessness program outcomes.

aligned_organizations.name: Austin YIMBY Action
aligned_organizations.relationship: Cross-movement ally. Housing supply is upstream of homelessness — every unit of housing built reduces the pressure that pushes people out. Their success at building housing directly reduces homelessness inflow. The YIMBY housing agenda and our safety accountability agenda are complementary.

aligned_organizations.name: Austin Abundance Project
aligned_organizations.relationship: Cross-movement ally. Family instability is a major pathway into homelessness. Their work on childcare, parental support, and family housing prevents the crises that lead to people losing housing. Family formation infrastructure is homelessness prevention infrastructure.

engagement_pipeline.awareness: Social media, neighborhood association meetings, local news coverage of public safety and homelessness data. Publish accountability dashboards — shelter outcomes tracker, APD response time maps, HSO progress reports — for organic discovery and sharing. Partner with Austin Monitor and Austin Bulldog for data-driven coverage.
engagement_pipeline.interest: Attend a public safety briefing. Read the shelter outcomes tracker. Check your district's APD response times. Review the HSO strategic plan progress report. Share your neighborhood's experience with the safety-services gap.
engagement_pipeline.action: Submit public comment at council on HSO strategic plan accountability. Testify at budget hearings for evidence-based public safety spending. Share district-level response time data with your council member. Attend HEAL community notification meetings. Contact council members about shelter funding and APD staffing.
engagement_pipeline.leadership: Join the data team tracking APD staffing and response times. Lead shelter outcomes research and facility-level analysis. Coordinate with Integral Care and ECHO on crisis response data. Represent us at HSO strategic plan review sessions. Present accountability findings at council hearings.

immediate.action: Track AISD recommended budget presentation to Board TOMORROW (MAY 21) — 215 FTE positions cut, larger class sizes confirmed. Directly impacts neighborhood safety and stability
Submit SpeakUpAustin comments on $766.5M bond — advocate for $131.1M public facilities allocation including purpose-built shelter to replace Marshalling Yard. BEATF presents May 28 (8 DAYS)
Analyze $497K Integral Care expansion — map how crisis health services capacity changes with new Homeless Health and Wellness Center funding
Track impact of removing embedded outreach workers from sweep teams — collect and report on whether the services linkage gap is being addressed

medium_term.action: Build and publish HSO strategic plan tracking dashboard with quarterly metrics
Publish first shelter outcomes report with facility-level data and population disaggregation
Build district-level APD staffing and response time dashboard — include the milestone that APD met its 10min44s Priority 0/1 target for first time since August 2023
Publish first HEAL transparency report with operation-level outcome data

strategic.action: Present comprehensive HSO accountability report at council budget hearings for FY2027
strategic.notes: First-year accountability report covering all five HSO focus areas. Timed to influence FY2027 budget decisions on the $101M identified funding gap. This is the credibility-building moment — demonstrate that rigorous, nonpartisan accountability improves public safety outcomes.

strategic.action: Publish annual public safety outcomes report integrating all four campaign data streams
strategic.notes: Combine HSO progress, shelter outcomes, APD staffing, and HEAL data into a single annual report. The integrated view shows how these issues connect — staffing affects response times, shelter outcomes affect encampment populations, HEAL operations depend on shelter capacity. Make the system visible.

strategic.action: Advocate for permanent public dashboards on homelessness spending and public safety outcomes
strategic.notes: Use the org's tracking infrastructure as a proof of concept for what the city should publish itself. The goal is to make our accountability work unnecessary by getting the city to publish this data proactively. If we succeed, we've institutionalized transparency.

bridge_building.opposition_steelman: Residents understandably fear that reform rhetoric can translate into weaker enforcement, slower emergency response, or unclear accountability for homelessness programs, and they want clear proof that public safety spending is producing measurable results.

cross_partisan_appeals.framing: Accountability is pro-safety: faster response times and effective interventions require transparent metrics.
cross_partisan_appeals.evidence: Performance dashboards and outcome tracking improve resource allocation and public trust across agencies.
cross_partisan_appeals.example_dialogue: "We can support first responders and still insist on clear goals, timelines, and outcomes."

cross_partisan_appeals.framing: Data transparency protects rights while improving service quality.
cross_partisan_appeals.evidence: Publishing disaggregated outcomes helps identify disparities and correct harmful practices.
cross_partisan_appeals.example_dialogue: "Better data is how we reduce harm and improve effectiveness at the same time."

cross_partisan_appeals.framing: Measure what works before expanding programs.
cross_partisan_appeals.evidence: Program evaluations can redirect spending from low-impact interventions to high-impact safety and housing supports.
cross_partisan_appeals.example_dialogue: "If taxpayers fund a program, taxpayers deserve to know what outcomes they bought."

common_ground: People deserve to feel safe in every neighborhood.
Emergency response should be fast, fair, and reliable.
Taxpayer-funded programs should publish measurable outcomes.
conversation_traps_to_avoid: Do not label all criticism as anti-reform or anti-police.
Do not reduce complex homelessness issues to a single agency failure.
Do not overclaim causality from short-term trend changes.
when_to_acknowledge_uncertainty: Attribution of year-to-year crime changes to any single intervention.
Long-term outcomes for newer homelessness initiatives.
Staffing pipelines affected by national labor-market conditions.
honest_limits: We do not run city departments directly.
We cannot guarantee immediate citywide response-time improvements.
We cannot solve homelessness without county, state, and nonprofit partners.
